hypothesis: Cross-sectional momentum persists due to behavioral underreaction to news and gradual information diffusion. Stocks with the highest 12-month returns (excluding the most recent month to avoid short-term reversal) continue to outperform over the next 1-3 months. The 1-month skip removes the well-documented short-term reversal effect.
universe: S&P 500 constituents — large-cap, high liquidity, sufficient borrowability for the short leg
entry_logic: Rank all S&P 500 stocks by cumulative 12-month return, skipping the most recent month (i.e., return from month t-12 to t-1). Long the top quintile (top 100 stocks), short the bottom quintile (bottom 100 stocks). Enter at month-end rebalance.
exit_logic: Monthly rebalance — exit positions that have left the top/bottom quintile. Positions are held exactly one month unless a stop is hit.
position_sizing: Equal-weight within each leg. Dollar-neutral construction (equal dollar long and short exposure). Volatility-scale if targeting a specific portfolio volatility (e.g., 10% annualized).
risk_management: Crash protection: if the S&P 500 1-month return is below -10%, reduce gross exposure by 50% until next rebalance. Max single position 2% of portfolio. Sector exposure: no single sector > 30% net. Stop-loss: exit any position that loses 8% from entry.
known_failure_modes: Momentum crashes: sharp market reversals (e.g., March 2009, March 2020) cause catastrophic losses — recent winners become crash victims, recent losers bounce hard.
Factor crowding: momentum is the most widely-known anomaly; as more capital chases it, the edge compresses and crash risk increases.
High turnover: monthly quintile rebalancing of 200 positions generates material transaction costs that erode edge.
Does not work in sideways or mean-reverting regimes — requires sustained trending to generate positive returns.
Short-term reversal contamination: without the 1-month skip, short-term reversal significantly degrades performance.

historical_performance.notes: The 1-month skip is critical — without it, Sharpe drops to ~0.8. The 2009 momentum crash produced drawdowns exceeding 50% in some implementations. Volatility scaling and crash protection help significantly. Robust across most periods except pronounced crash episodes.

references: Jegadeesh & Titman (1993) - Returns to Buying Winners and Selling Losers, Journal of Finance
Stefan Jansen - ML for Algorithmic Trading, Chapter 4
Asness, Moskowitz & Pedersen (2013) - Value and Momentum Everywhere, Journal of Finance
